title: Predicting Mortality in Sepsis: The Role of Dynamic Biomarker Changes and Clinical Scores—A Retrospective Cohort Study

sample_size.source_quote: The study cohort consisted of 138 patients with sepsis, of which 63 (45.7%) were female and 75 (54.3%) were male.

patient_age_mean.source_quote: The median age was 75 years (IQR 69–82), with the non-survivors being significantly older than the survivors (median of 79 vs. 72 years, p < 0.05).

sofa_score_baseline.source_quote: The median SOFA score at the baseline was 5 (IQR of 4–7), with the non-survivors exhibiting a higher median score of 6 compared to 4 in the survivors (p < 0.05).

mortality_predictors.source_quote: the non-survivors displayed significantly higher lactate levels (median 3.37 mmol/L vs. 2.71 mmol/L, p < 0.05)

mortality_predictors.model_context: adjusted for CCI, NLR, baseline SOFA score
mortality_predictors.source_quote: Our findings revealed that the Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI), Neutrophil-to-Lymphocyte Ratio (NLR), baseline lactate level (LAC_Base), and baseline SOFA score (SOFA_Base) were significant predictors of mortality. Notably, for each one-unit increase in the CCI, the odds of mortality increased by 31.6%, while a one-unit increase in the NLR was associated with a 47% increase in the odds of mortality. The baseline lactate level emerged as a particularly strong predictor, with each one-unit increase more than doubling the odds of mortality.

mortality_predictors.timing: percentage change from baseline to 72 hours (M3)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: decreases in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels were significantly associated with a reduced 30-day mortality. Specifically, for each 1% decrease in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels, from baseline to M3, the odds of 30-day mortality decreased. This is reflected in the odds ratios of 0.948, 0.762, and 0.887, respectively.

mortality_predictors.timing: percentage change from baseline to 72 hours (M3)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: The AUCs for the percentage decreases in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels, from baseline to M3 (PCT_Delta: 0.843, CRP_Delta: 0.903, and LAC_Delta: 0.703), were notably higher than those for their corresponding baseline values (PCT_Base: 0.478, CRP_Base: 0.540, and LAC_Base: 0.689).

mortality_predictors.source_quote: In contrast, age, white blood cell count (WBC), baseline PCT, and baseline CRP did not exhibit significant associations with 30-day mortality in this model.

The AUCs for the percentage decreases in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels, from baseline to M3 (PCT_Delta: 0.843, CRP_Delta: 0.903, and LAC_Delta: 0.703), were notably higher than those for their corresponding baseline values (PCT_Base: 0.478, CRP_Base: 0.540, and LAC_Base: 0.689).

Specifically, for each 1% decrease in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels, from baseline to M3, the odds of 30-day mortality decreased. mortality_predictors.timing: percentage change from baseline to 72 hours (M3)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: The AUCs for the percentage decreases in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels, from baseline to M3 (PCT_Delta: 0.843, CRP_Delta: 0.903, and LAC_Delta: 0.703), were notably higher than those for their corresponding baseline values (PCT_Base: 0.478, CRP_Base: 0.540, and LAC_Base: 0.689).
The median SOFA score at the baseline was 5 (IQR of 4–7), with the non-survivors exhibiting a higher median score of 6 compared to 4 in the survivors (p < 0.05).

the baseline SOFA score (SOFA_Base) were significant predictors of mortality. Notably, for each one-unit increase in the CCI, the odds of mortality increased by 31.6%, while a one-unit increase in the NLR was associated with a 47% increase in the odds of mortality. The baseline lactate level emerged as a particularly strong predictor, with each one-unit increase more than doubling the odds of mortality. Similarly, the baseline SOFA score demonstrated a substantial impact, with each one-unit increase leading to a 73.5% increase in the odds of mortality.

Among the baseline predictors, the SOFA score exhibited the highest discriminatory power (AUC = 0.762), followed by age (0.730) and NLR (0.716), suggesting a moderate ability to distinguish between the survivors and non-survivors.

mortality_predictors.timing: percentage change from baseline to 72 hours (M3)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: decreases in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels were significantly associated with a reduced 30-day mortality. Specifically, for each 1% decrease in the PCT, CRP, and LAC levels, from baseline to M3, the odds of 30-day mortality decreased. This is reflected in the odds ratios of 0.948, 0.762, and 0.887, respectively. To illustrate this, the odds ratio of 0.762 for the CRP_Delta signifies that for every 1% decrease in CRP levels over the first three days, the odds of mortality are reduced by approximately 24% (1 −0.762 = 0.238), holding other factors constant.

the non-survivors displayed significantly higher lactate levels (median 3.37 mmol/L vs. 2.71 mmol/L, p < 0.05) and a higher Neutrophil-to-Lymphocyte Ratio (NLR) (median 9.4 vs. 7.58, p < 0.05) compared to the survivors.
a one-unit increase in the NLR was associated with a 47% increase in the odds of mortality.

Among the baseline predictors, the SOFA score exhibited the highest discriminatory power (AUC = 0.762), followed by age (0.730) and NLR (0.716), suggesting a moderate ability to distinguish between the survivors and non-survivors.
